There are days when the world sucks more than others. On the occasion of the premiere of Woman King, National Geographic has dedicated a face lift, or rather a blood wash, to this tribe of slavers who were the Dahomey, more specifically to the fanatic, slit throat fat women who blindly served their wren. The horrors that these women practiced had no limit, from annihilating villages at night, carrying out monstrous human sacrifices, and even hunting the elephants in the area to extinction. This is how they were spent by a kingdom whose main trade was the sale of slaves to the hungry European powers, but also for its own use, because in this kingdom, whose throne was decorated with human skulls, about the 90% were slaves. None of this will be seen in the movie, nor in the pathetic Geographic article, where you can read "larger than life", "empowered" and other paid nonsense. We are going downhill if we make heros of murderers, idols of the psychopaths and liberators of the slavers. The legend of the invincible female warriors, who made up only a sixth of the royal army and who had already suffered many defeats at the hands of other tribes, ended when they messed with the French, who did not have a hard time crushing them: heavily outnumbered they suffered hardly any casualties; in contrast,  the Dahomey amazons were all but wiped out. I guess the next movie will be about a cannibalistic, pederast hero, as long as he's colored, because that's the only thing left to justify in these deranged times.

It’s one thing to fabricate history about a side you can root for. It’s another to make bad people who were not good people look like “good” people. They were awful slavers and sacrificed human beings. Some people love to talk about how whites revised history (and once upon a time they did) but look at how they are revising and cherry picking the things they like about these barbaric people who were actively engaged in practicing slavery and human sacrifice. There is nothing to praise here. Even if the performances are good and it’s technically well made. I’m glad to hear that they at least acknowledge some things but they still attempt to give these people humanity which is impossible to empathize with if you know basic history about the slave trade and how black people came into the clutches of white slavers. It didn’t start with white people. Reality may not be convenient to hear but it is what it is. And anyone who is blind will have to come to terms with the fact that slavery isn’t the brainchild of white people but actually a practice that was very prevalent worldwide across all cultures.

The Dahomey Amazons (Fon: "Agojie", "Agoji", "Mino", or "Minon") were a Fon all-female military regiment of the Kingdom of Dahomey which existed from the 1600s until 1904.
The emergence of an all-female military regiment was the result of Dahomey's male population facing high casualties in the increasingly frequent violence and warfare with neighbouring West African states. This led to Dahomey being one of the leading tribes in the slave trade with the Oyo Empire, which used slaves for commodity exchange in West Africa until the British Empire brought an end to the slave trade in the region. The lack of men likely led the kings of Dahomey to recruit women into the army.[2]

“The Woman King,” starring Viola Davis, is a new historical epic inspired by the Agojie, an all-female military force serving the West African Kingdom of Dahomey in the 19th century. The film is centered around a female general and her Amazon unit as they train for and ultimately battle against an army that is looking to change their way of life. The only problem with this story, besides the fact that the theme is devoid of originality and taste, is that the Kingdom of Dahomey was famously involved in the procurement of African slaves for Europeans and themselves.  The kingdom was so prolific in the slave trade during the 19th century that according to an article in the Journal of African History by a professor from the University of Stirling, the British Empire had to pressure Dahomey “to end the slave trade, which culminated at the end of 1851 in a naval blockade of Dahomey.” Not only did the Kingdom of Dahomey directly contribute to the slave trade, but they actually had to be forcefully stopped from participating.

Okay so the story of a country with a pretty violent history regarding its African neighbours, a significant involvement in the slave trade, a royal guard of women that were all legally the king's wives and underwent genital mutilation and so much as laying a hand on them was punishable by death, and seemingly a conflict with Europeans.

Now this conflict with Europeans could really be one of two things, the British Blockade to stop the Atlantic Slave Trade, which the King of Dahomay defended as a vital part of their culture.

Or this is the story of the first or second war with the french.
In the first war, they raided and attacked a French protecturate, bringing the French into a war to defend their ally, they won after a few months and established a treaty.

Or this is the second war, which lead to french Colonization which occurred after dahomey renounced a treaty with the French, raided several french protectorates, leading to France concluding that the best way to put an end to the situation was a military takeover, they won the war in about two years, maybe a little less, understandably so, as the Dahomey frankly didnt stand a chance, especially with the notable defeat of the Dahomey Amazons, the French engaged them in a bayonet charge, the Dahomey loosing 86 regulars and 417 Amazon's for 6 french soldiers.

The French exiled the current king, put his brother in charge, then exiled him as well in place of direct french administration, though the brother was later allowed to return and live in the country again, though was not allowed to roam freely in the capital.

The Monarchy was maintained however and is still present. As well as the De Souza family which rose to prominence when Francisco Félix de Sousa helped king Ghezo attain power in a coup de ta.

"Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland. The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey.

Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

There was a history of human sacrifice using slaves."

I am very glad to FINALLY see some truth about the slave trade being revealed. In 1990 my high school teacher in Houston made us read about the Kingdom of Dahomey and how they sold slaves to the Europeans. We were made to read this to show the lies being written in government funded history books.

"How accurate is The Woman King?" we learned that in real life, the Dahomey are much more the villains than the heroes. The Kingdom of Dahomey was a bloodthirsty society bent on conquest. It was customary for the Dahomey to return home with the rotting heads and genitals of those they killed in battle. They conquered neighboring African states and took their citizens as slaves, selling many in the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for items like rifles, tobacco, and alcohol. Many of the slaves they sold ended up in America. They also kept some slaves for themselves to work on royal plantations. The business of slavery is what brought Dahomey most of its wealth. For them, it very much came down to either enslave others or become enslaved yourself.

The Agojie (women warriors) fought in slave raids along with the male fighters.  There are accounts of Dahomey warriors conducting slave raids on villages where they cut the heads off of the elderly and rip the bottom jaw bones off others. During the raids, they'd burn the villages to the ground. Those who they let live, including the children, were taken captive and sold as slaves. The movie strategically downplays this part of Dahomey's history, so as to not complicate the story with the truth.

Each year in Dahomey, roughly 500 slaves and criminals were mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the religious ceremonies of a festival known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey. Most were sacrificed by way of decapitation, a method of killing widely used by the Dahomean kings. The 1727 Annual Customs of the Dahomey ceremony reportedly saw as many as 4,000 people sacrificed.

This very real History has to do with my Angola People so much that I have descendants of this Queen Mzinga and I take the name of Matamba too.

The kingdoms of Ndongo and Matamba were important African states existing before the arrival of the Portuguese; a hierarchical and organized society, with domain of commerce, metallurgy, agriculture. Currently, they are part of what we now know as Angola.

Nzinga was born between 1581/82, years after the arrival in the region of the Portuguese navigator Paulo Dias de Novais (1560) and the foundation of the city of Luanda (1575), in a context of struggles and resistance against Portuguese occupation. From 1580, the Portuguese intensified the slave trade, went to war against Ndongo and conquered the entire region. Daughter of Ngola Mbande Kiluanji, King of Ndongo, proved to be an excellent negotiator when her brother, King Ngola Mabande's successor, sent her to Luanda, one of the largest export centers for enslaved people on the African continent, in order to negotiate a peace treaty. that would establish respect for the sovereignty of the kingdom. Nzinga knew the Portuguese language and culture well, thanks to her contacts with missionaries and traders who passed through Ndongo.

Despite the period of peace that followed the signing of the treaty, conflicts returned with the replacement of the Portuguese viceroy by a successor who did not respect his commitments. With the death of Ngola Mbande, her brother, Nzinga became queen; he imposed his authority on local chiefs, conquered the neighboring kingdom of Matamba, and became a strong political figure in the region.

For four decades, Nzinga a Mbande represented the resistance of Ndongo and allowed to mitigate Portuguese projects in the region, through guerrilla and espionage tactics, directing military operations, but also through diplomacy, since she was an expert negotiator. He made alliances with the King of Congo and with the Dutch in order to defend his kingdom from Portuguese threats .............In this context, the film had to bear the NAME OF QUEEN MZINGA So that everyone could know and to remain anonymous this strong WARRIOR AMEM VIVA AFRICA-ANGOLA and thanks to the Producer for making this film that has a very intense story.

"Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland. The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey. Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey."

As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.

Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

During the ceremony, around 500 prisoners would be sacrificed. As many as 4,000 were reported killed In one of these ceremonies in 1727.] Most of the victims were sacrificed through decapitation, a tradition widely used by Dahomean kings, and the literal translation for the Fon name for the ceremony Xwetanu is "yearly head business". In later years this ceremony also included the spilling of human blood from the sacrificed.

The Amazons participated in one major battle: Cotonou, where thousands of Dahomey (including many Amazons) charged the French lines and engaged the defenders in hand-to-hand combat. The Amazons were decisively crushed, with several hundred Dahomey troops being gunned down. Reportedly 129 Dahomey were killed in melee combat within the French lines.

Against a military unit with decidedly superior weaponry and a longer bayonet, however, the Dahomey Amazons could not prevail. During a battle with French soldiers at Adegon on 6 October during the second war, the bulk of the Amazon corps were wiped out in a matter of hours in hand-to-hand combat after the French engaged them with a bayonet charge. The Dahomey lost 86 regulars and 417 Dahomey Amazons, with nearly all of those deaths being inflicted by bayonets; the French lost 6 soldiers.

The woke agenda is at it again with a horribly skewed interpretation of the Dahomey kingdom of Africa in an effort to take advantage of people’s ignorance of the historically accurate subject matter. Supporting this movie is supporting slavery, but woke hollywood wants to twist it into a female empowerment flick about fighting slavery when in reality the Dahomey were arrogant slave traders that conducted brutal human sacrifices. A simple wikipedia search of the Dahomey kingdom will tell you the truth:
“ The Kingdom of Dahomey was an important regional power that had an organized domestic economy built on conquest and slave labor. The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves.[2] As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.[5][6] Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.[2][6] The Annual Customs of Dahomey involved significant collection and distribution of gifts and tribute, religious Vodun ceremonies, military parades, and discussions by dignitaries about the future for the kingdom.

In the 1840s, Dahomey began to face decline with British pressure to abolish the slave trade, which included the British Royal Navy imposing a naval blockade against the kingdom and enforcing anti-slavery patrols near its coast.[7] During this time period, Dahomey was also weakened by military defeat from Abeokuta, a Yoruba city-state which was founded as a safe haven for refugees escaping slave raids from Dahomey.“

Dahomey was organized for war, not only to expand its boundaries but also to take captives as slaves. Slaves were either sold to the Europeans in exchange for weapons or kept to work the royal plantations that supplied food for the army and court.

The Dahomey Kingdom became known to European traders at this time as a major source of slaves in the slave trade at Allada and Whydah.

In 1851-1852, the British imposed a naval blockade on the ports of Dahomey in order to force them to end the slave trade. In January 1852, Ghezo accepted a treaty with the British ending the export of slaves from Dahomey.

he Kingdom of Dahomey was an important regional power that had an organized domestic economy built on conquest and slave labor, significant international trade and diplomatic relations with Europeans, a centralized administration, taxation systems, and an organized military.

There were two Franco-Dahomean wars, which ended in 1894 with France taking over. The Kingdom of Dahomey eventually became French Dahomey, a colony, ten years later.

Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland. The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey.

Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

In reality the Dahomey are much more the villains than the heroes. The Kingdom of Dahomey was a bloodthirsty society bent on conquest. It was customary for the Dahomey to return home with the rotting heads and genitals of those they killed in battle. They conquered neighboring African states and took their citizens as slaves, selling many in the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for items like rifles, tobacco, and alcohol. Many of the slaves they sold ended up in America. They also kept some slaves for themselves to work on royal plantations. The business of slavery is what brought Dahomey most of its wealth. For them, it very much came down to either enslave others or become enslaved yourself. In movie, Nanisca (Viola Davis) is the general of the Agojie (Dahomey Amazons). While she appears to be almost entirely fictional, French naval officer Jean Bayol, who visited the region in December 1889, wrote of watching a teenage recruit named Nanisca, "who had not yet killed anyone." He describes her approaching a young prisoner sitting bound in a basket. Nanisca took her sword in both hands and swung three times, almost entirely decapitating the prisoner. She then cut the remaining bit of flesh that held the head to the trunk and "squeezed the blood off her weapon and swallowed it." While Viola Davis' character is much older, it's possible her name was inspired by the teenage Nanisca the French officer observed.

Don’t be fooled by Hollywood and their attempt to rewrite history. The main character Nanisca, played by Viola Davis, is almost entirely fictional. All but the name is accurate. Nanisca was a real person, who was witnessed by a French naval officer, brutally kill a bound prisoner and drink their blood in 1889.
The all female warriors were known as the Agojie from the Kingdom of Dahomey who were a bloodthirsty group of female warriors commanded by King Ghezo, played by John Boyega. King Ghezo was an active slave owner and trader which brought much wealth into the Kingdom of Dahomey. The Agojie would raid, slaughter, and capture neighboring tribes to capture and sell. 
So, however the story Hollywood feels they want to tell here, the Kingdom of Dahomey is the real villain. They sent many slaves to America. Although the Agojie were strong and considered admirable in combat, it was under the pretense of the inhumane rule of King Ghezo. It was the British who blocked ports in Dahomey to end the Atlantic slave trade in the 1850’s which was the only real conflict Dahomey had with the Europeans at that time.

Any serious African historian knows what happened between France and Dahomey and France itself can't claim to have come to Dahomey to abolish any slavery. In 1890 transatlantic slavery hardly existed anymore, even in Dahomey. France came to invade a free country under the pretext of protecting Porto Novo from its neighbor. France wanted to seize Dahomey in order not to be outdone by the British in the colonial conquest. The conflict really started when France claimed the city of Cotonou as its own. It found an organized army composed in part of women, the Amazones, in its path. France used its technical superiority to kill, massacre and pillage a foreign country. Benin has just recovered some of its works of art returned by France. Go and see its works, which Picasso himself was inspired by, to see if they are the work of a savage people. "How accurate is The Woman King?" we learned that in real life, the Dahomey are much more the villains than the heroes. The Kingdom of Dahomey was a bloodthirsty society bent on conquest. It was customary for the Dahomey to return home with the rotting heads and genitals of those they killed in battle. They conquered neighboring African states and took their citizens as slaves, selling many in the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for items like rifles, tobacco, and alcohol. Many of the slaves they sold ended up in America. They also kept some slaves for themselves to work on royal plantations. The business of slavery is what brought Dahomey most of its wealth. For them, it very much came down to either enslave others or become enslaved yourself.

The Agojie (women warriors) fought in slave raids along with the male fighters.  There are accounts of Dahomey warriors conducting slave raids on villages where they cut the heads off of the elderly and rip the bottom jaw bones off others. During the raids, they'd burn the villages to the ground. Those who they let live, including the children, were taken captive and sold as slaves. The movie strategically downplays this part of Dahomey's history, so as to not complicate the story with the truth.

Each year in Dahomey, roughly 500 slaves and criminals were mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the religious ceremonies of a festival known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey. Most were sacrificed by way of decapitation, a method of killing widely used by the Dahomean kings. The 1727 Annual Customs of the Dahomey ceremony reportedly saw as many as 4,000 people sacrificed.

Here is the real Info:

Dahomey was a kingdom that conquered other African states/tribes and enslaved their citizens to sell in the slave trade, they were known to be merciless in their slave raiding, and most of the kingdom's wealth was derived from slavery. Although Wikipedia sources misleads the reader into thinking they only started slave trading because of the Portuguese, slavery, slave raiding and slave trading was common since the 7th century A.D when contact with Islamic Berbers was made. 
They also created and controlled human breeding farms, many think this due to King Agaja adding human sacrifice into custom and a large number of infants was needed, won't go into details but cannibalism was also involved with the sacrifices. 

In the film the women want to stop the slave trading, this did not happen, both men and women embraced slavery, the Agojie (female warriors) were actually mostly slave raiders.

The Agojie (female warriors) were created  because they were the "unwanted" meaning no one wanted to marry or have sex with them, they couldn't be used as prostitutes, King Houegbadja needed fodder for elephant hunting which was extremely dangerous and had a high mortality rate, instead of using his precious seasoned male warriors, he used these "unwanted women" and put them in use. Under Ghezo who was very militaristic, believed that the greater the number of soldiers the better the results, any women could join in this new band of warriors,  they were easily brainwashed into believing that they were men, marriage and sex was also banned.

I can see here many uninformed people who believe themselves to be historians after reading two lines on Wikipedia, who constently fire the kingdom of Dahomey, even claiming that its Amazons never fought for freedom but rather to continue slavery. Some have even come to consider the English and French colonizers as heroes: Oh my God! Let's read the facts about the French wars against the kingdom of Dahomey from 1890 onwards, they were purely colonial wars against the background of competition between Europeans to better share Africa Land. They decide that in a Berlin's Conference on 1885. Certainly, Dahomey participated in the triangular trade like many other African coastal kingdoms that could not resist Europe's fury to deport blacks to America.  The European powers organized a great insecurity on the African continent, stirring up tribal wars to secure more easly "ebony wood", slaves. Long before the British abolished slavery ( after losing their colony in the United States and no longer being able to make a profit from this trade) in 1833, King Adandozan of Dahomey (1797-1818) had already wanted to put an end to his kingdom's involvement in the slave trade, but the slave-owning countries sent him the Portuguese-Brazilian Francisco de Souza, who overthrew Adandozan and installed his brother Prince Guézo in his place. Franscico de Souza had almost become a vice-king, the promoter of the slave trade. Guézo won his throne thanks to him...
In short, in 1890 the abolition of transatlantic slavery was already a reality. The kings of Dahomey, who swore an oath at their enthronement to expand the kingdom, were instead waging wars of conquest, as all the great empires had done. Dahomey did not wage war against England. It fought against France who wanted to annex its territory and found great resistance, thanks in particular to the Amazons who effectively fought for the independence of their country. The protection of the kingdom of Porto-Novo was only a pretext, the nice proof is that after having defeated Behanzin after 4 years of struggle, France will ignore the kingdom of Abomey as much as that of Porto-Novo to make itself absolute master of the country. As for the losses of the French Army, I don't know where you got the number of  6 persons, even Wikipedia indicates 85 dead, only for the Second Dahomey War. Don't forget that the French reports often only count the white soldiers who fell on the battlefield, but the French army that attacked Dahomey was largely composed of Senegalese riflemen and other african soldiers, not to mention the 500 warriors provided by the king of Porto-Novo...
The interest of this film is not the history of Dahomey but it aims to show that Africa also wrote the history: in an African kingdom the woman made herself the equal of the man at the time when her fellow men were everywhere inferiorized in the world. In her book "Wives of the Leopard" about the Amazons of Dahomey, the American historian, author and researcher Edna G. Bay wrote:   "At that time I did not see a single place on earth where women had as much power as in Dahomey". Bay wrote, "At the time I did not see a single place on earth where women had as much power as in Dahomey''. This wasn't a legend like the Greeks, it was a reality, the army of Dahomey was composed in part of women who were generals, commanders, officers...This army corps was not founded to chase slaves, it was founded by Queen Tassi Hangbé between 1708 and 1711 precisely to show that women could equal and even surpass men in everything, especially in bravery, which seemed insane at that time in Europe. Yes, the Agojies or Minos of Dahomey were a glory for Africa, even if they were as obedient soldiers participated in some of the excesses of their sovereigns.

The Woman King is revisionist history.

The Kingdom of Dahomey was a brutal militaristic society based on conquest and enslavement of other Africans. They also committed mass ritual executions of slaves. 
The film attempts to portray the all-female military unit (which did exist) of the Kingdom as sympathetic and heroic. They fight against the evil European colonizers. 

In reality, during the time period that the film is set, this unit was conquering and enslaving other Africans and did not engage in warfare with Europeans, but engaged in the slave trade with them. Dahomey only engaged in warfare with Europeans AFTER slavery was outlawed in Europe and this was a territorial war. The female warriors of Dahomey did eventually oppose selling their slaves to Europeans, but did not advocate for them to be free. They still supported those Africans being enslaved within Dahomey, they only opposed them being sold to others. 
The Amazons of Dahomey may have been heroic in how they fought but they certainly were not heroic in what they fought FOR. Quite simply, they fought for the evil of the enslavement and slaughter of other African people. They were villians not heroes.

Dahomey was a country whose culture was formed to a large extent by the Atlantic Slave Trade. The violence that demand for slaves created meant it became increasingly militarised and the profits of that trade meant it fought it's way to the coast to take advantage. This was a relatively expected route for growth of a West African slaving Kingdom. Interior Kingdoms which were raided for slaves, would grow larger to defend themselves and then would attempt to fight to the coast to get into the slave trading game themselves while the coastal Kingdoms would attempt to prevent them doing so. The Ashanti in Ghana had a similar history.

Dahomey and the European traders were on largely equal terms within West Africa, due to the small number of the latter. Dahomey won a significant battle against European traders in 1728, only two years after losing so badly to the Oyo. And while the Dutch defeated them in the 1730s, that was as a small part of an Army primarily made up of other Africans. The European traders were an important part of Dahomean politics but they. 

When the British asked Dahomey to stop selling slaves in 1849, They made it very clear that he feared his own slave traders more than he feared the British.

By the end of the Second Franco-Dahomean War, special units of the Amazons were being assigned specifically to target French officers.[18] After several battles, the French prevailed in the Second Franco-Dahomean War and put an end to the independent Dahomean kingdom. French soldiers, particularly of the French Foreign Legion, were impressed by the boldness of the Amazons and later wrote about their "incredible courage and audacity" in combat.[12]

Against a military unit with decidedly superior weaponry and a longer bayonet, however, the Dahomey Amazons could not prevail.[12] During a battle with French soldiers at Adegon on 6 October during the second war, the bulk of the Amazon corps were wiped out in a matter of hours in hand-to-hand combat after the French engaged them with a bayonet charge.[19] The Dahomey lost 86 regulars and 417 Dahomey Amazons, with nearly all of those deaths being inflicted by bayonets; the French lost 6 soldiers.

Most of the comments are from people who THINK they know history but clearly don't. As an American, I can definitely say the American school system did a poor job of explaining nuance when it came to history. There were hardly ever "good" or "bad" in history. Many people did whatever they had to do to survive. It's hard to explain that in the context of 2022. People need to stop seeing history through the lens of 2022. You will never understand anything if you do that.  We will never understand how certain decisions were made in the context of the time these people were living in.  

Once again - for people who seem to not understand - AFRICA IS A CONTINENT. It has over 50 different countries and many, many tribes and ethnic groups. All the different ethnic groups did not see themselves as the same. No matter whatever you were taught - it's not the truth.  This idea that they were "enslaving their own people" is fallacy. It doesn't matter that in 2022 you think all brown skin people are the same - they weren't. They all had different cultures/customs/languages, etc. 

Let's get down to business since everyone likes to play race wars with this - slavery has existed since the beginning of time. OK. Let's make this very clear. It was one of the ways you built an economy. There was no concept of black, white, Asian, etc. until the late 16th century. This is the puzzle piece many people miss on telling the truth about slavery. Slavery was not racial at all at yet. At one time, people mostly enslaved neighboring ethnic groups - they would enslave people after wars. It was kind of their "prize" for conquering the land. The concept of "race" as we think of it in 2022 didn't exist yet. I don't know why people neglect to mention this very important fact. 

The concept of enslaving someone based on what we think of as "race" in today's terms didn't come until much later. It was a very new concept that unfortunately spilled into North America (mainly what would become the USA). 

Slavery is not "good". Let's be clear. But, also we need to make a distinction that there is a HUGE difference between people enslaved others as a means of survival (a way to make money/financial/building an economy) vs creating a caste system based on skin tone to determine who deserves to be enslaved.

In the 1840s, Dahomey began to face decline with British pressure to abolish the slave trade, which included the British Royal Navy imposing a naval blockade against the kingdom and enforcing anti-slavery patrols near its coast. During this time period, Dahomey was also weakened by military defeat from Abeokuta, a Yoruba city-state which was founded as a safe haven for refugees escaping slave raids from Dahomey. Dahomey later began experiencing territorial tensions with France which led to the First Franco-Dahomean War in 1890, resulting in French victory. The kingdom finally fell in 1894 when the last king, Béhanzin, was defeated by France in the Second Franco-Dahomean War, leading to the country being annexed into French West Africa as the colony of French Dahomey.

(From Wikipedia)

The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves. As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.

Amazons of Black Sparta, 2nd Edition: The Women Warriors of Dahomey: "The only thoroughly documented Amazons in world history are the women warriors of Dahomey, an eighteenth- and nineteenth-century Western African kingdom. Once dubbed a ‘small black Sparta,’ residents of Dahomey shared with the Spartans an intense militarism and sense of collectivism. Moreover, the women of both kingdoms prided themselves on bodies hardened from childhood by rigorous physical exercise. But Spartan women kept in shape to breed male warriors, Dahomean Amazons to kill them. Originally palace guards, the Amazons had evolved by the 1760s into professional troops armed mainly with muskets, machetes and clubs. By the 1840s their numbers had grown to 6,000. The Amazons served under female officers and had their own bands, flags and insignia: they outdrilled, outshot and outfought men, became frontline troops and fought tenaciously and with great valor till the kingdom’s defeat by France in 1892."

Out of the 15 million Africans sent to the Americas, two million passed through the Kingdom of Dahomey in Ouidah, Benin. There is now a museum and monument to this point of transit. Based on the historical record, General Nanisca might have died as a young woman. However, while this movie is about maintaining independence from an encroaching European power that eventually prevailed in modern Benin, the Dahomey kingdom was heavily dependent on the trans-Atlantic slave trade. Europeans didn't penetrate the African interior till the late nineteenth century. By that time, the British Navy had all but stopped the movement of people across the Atlantic and the United States had ended chattel slavery. When the Dahomey would capture other Africans and sell them to the Europeans, they had them perform a ritual around the "Tree of Forgetfulness." That tree no longer exists, but you can find a plaque and statue marking where it once stood in Ouidah, Benin. The Dahomey royals feared that the spirits of the enslaved would haunt them if they didn't perform this ritual before heading to the Americas.

I urge everyone to read up on the Kingdom of Dahomey before celebrating this film. Prepare to discover the shocking reality of slavery and how it was not singlehandedly operated by European nations. The complicity of this crime runs deep and well beyond skin colour. Don’t let the media and Hollywood sanitize history.

"The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves.[2] As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.[5][6] Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.[2][6] The Annual Customs of Dahomey involved significant collection and distribution of gifts and tribute, religious Vodun ceremonies, military parades, and discussions by dignitaries about the future for the kingdom."

Im guessing they probably also dont explore the fact Dahomey was a notorious slave kingdom, who refused too give up the practice until the British invaded and made them, and indeed half the reason many of the local African powers ended up as British protectorates such as Nigeria was explicitly to protect them from Dahomey slave raids. 

When asked to end slavery by the British, their king said:
"The slave trade has been the ruling principle of my people. It is the source of their glory and wealth. Their songs celebrate their victories and the mother lulls the child to sleep with notes of triumph over an enemy reduced to slavery."

In 1849 Dahomey even presented a slave girl as a gift to a British captain who was on a diplomatic mission there. She was later freed, became of ward of Queen Victoria and went under the name Sara Forbes Bonetta.

Oh, Lord. Did the writers believe all of us were ignorant of history? The Dahomey kingdom (now Bénin) is one of the most document history in Africa.
"The Kingdom of Dahomey was an important regional power that had an organized domestic economy built on conquest and slave labor,[3] significant international trade and diplomatic relations with Europeans, a centralized administration, taxation systems, and an organized military. Notable in the kingdom were significant artwork, an all-female military unit called the Dahomey Amazons by European observers, and the elaborate religious practices of Vodun.[4]

The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves.[2] As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.[5][6] Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey."

>The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves.[2] As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.[5][6] Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.[2][6] The Annual Customs of Dahomey involved significant collection and distribution of gifts and tribute, religious Vodun ceremonies, military parades, and discussions by dignitaries about the future for the kingdom.

>In the 1840s, Dahomey began to face decline with British pressure to abolish the slave trade, which included the British Royal Navy imposing a naval blockade against the kingdom and enforcing anti-slavery patrols near its coast.[7] During this time period, Dahomey was also weakened by military defeat from Abeokuta, a Yoruba city-state which was founded as a safe haven for refugees escaping slave raids from Dahomey.[8] Dahomey later began experiencing territorial tensions with France which led to the First Franco-Dahomean War in 1890, resulting in French victory. The kingdom finally fell in 1894 when the last king, Béhanzin, was defeated by France in the Second Franco-Dahomean War, leading to the country being annexed into French West Africa as the colony of French Dahomey.

Dahomey had communities that were considered slave havens that protected people from colonial forces. It was people selectively chosen through their connection to a Dahomey individual or when American supply lines closed down and they wanted to hold people just in case. However, Dahomey people were only able to distinguish themselves from other African peoples by providing slaves themselves and so horrible outcomes stemmed from desperate measures to adapt when Europeans had surpassed them as power-brokers in the region. The slave-trade would have existed with or without the Dahomey empire, but their history with France and England should be viewed as provoked and complicit involvement with Western intentions for a slave market that acted to facilitate an inevitable outcome for Europe sooner because they were better at gathering slaves. There is not a lot of moral leadership you can glean from them, but knowing the desperation and arguable pragmatism if you remove immoral attribute from your criteria of what defines pragmatism. I think that and their religion (verdure? …cannot remember) make them comparably powerful people worth learning about. The Kingdom of Dehomey and the Suppression of the slave trade (1852-1880). Actual History vs a movie......
Two major changes occurred in the 1840s and 1850s which significantly altered politics in Dahomey. First, the British who had been a major purchaser of slaves began taking an active stance in abolishing the slave trade in the 1830s. They sent multiple diplomatic parties to Ghezo to try and convince him to end Dahomey’s participation in the trade, all of these were rebuffed with Ghezo worried of the political consequences of ending such trade. Second, the city of Abeokuta was founded in 1825 and rose to prominence as a safe haven for people to be safe from the slave raids by Dahomey. In 1844, Dahomey and Abeokuta went to war and Abeokuta was victorious. Other violence in the early 1850s further cemented Abeokuta’s challenge to Dahomey’s economic control in the region....Internally, the pressure resulted in a number of changes. Ghezo rejected British requests for ending the slave trade, but at the same time began expanded significantly the palm oil trade as an economic alternative. Politically, the debate became centered around two political factions: the Elephant and the Fly. The Elephant, connected with Ghezo, high-profile political leaders, and the creole slave traders like the family of De Sousa, pushed for continued activity in the slave trade and resistance to British pressure. The Fly faction, in contrast, was a loose collection of palm oil producers and some chieftains, which supported accommodation with Abeokuta and the British in order to expand palm oil trade. At the policy and war debates held at the Annual Customs these two factions held a number of tense discussions about the future of the Kingdom of Dahomey.
In 1851-1852, the British imposed a naval blockade on the ports of Dahomey in order to force them to end the slave trade. In January 1852, Ghezo accepted a treaty with the British ending the export of slaves from Dahomey. In the same year and the following one, Ghezo suspended large-scale military campaigns and human sacrifice in the kingdom. However political pressure contributed to the resumption of slave trading and large scale military action in 1857 and 1858. Ghezo was assassinated by a sniper associated with Abeokuta and large scale warfare between the two states resumed in 1864. This one ended again in the favor of Abeokuta and the result was that the slave trade could not be significantly reestablished to its 1850 level. The power of slave traders in the empire decreased and the palm oil trade became a more significant part of the economy.

First Franco-Dahomean War: "The Amazons participated in one major battle: Cotonou, where thousands of Dahomey (including many Amazons) charged the French lines and engaged the defenders in hand-to-hand combat.The Amazons were decisively crushed, with several hundred Dahomey troops being gunned down" (Wikipedia, Dahomey Amazons). Second Franco-Dahomean War: "Against a military unit with decidedly superior male soldiers equipped with better weaponry and longer bayonets, the Dahomey Amazons were again crushed. "Some news publications, including The Daily Signal and the Washington Examiner, have questioned and criticized the historical accuracy of the film for failing to recognize and address the realities of the Kingdom of Dahomey. Rather than being a kingdom fighting for pan-African unity and resistance to foreign invasion, in truth the conflicts depicted centered around the slave trade. The Kingdom of Dahomey was heavily invested in capturing and selling Africans slaves. The slave trade was a cornerstone of their wealth and prestige in the area, and the military of the Kingdom routinely engaged in raids and warfare for the explicit purpose of selling and keeping captives. For example, historian Martin Meredith quotes Ghezo (portrayed by Boyega) telling the British, "The slave trade has been the ruling principle of my people. It is the source of their glory and wealth. Their songs celebrate their victories and the mother lulls the child to sleep with notes of triumph over an enemy reduced to slavery."

This person was not "king" by any sense of the word. 
The Dahomey Amazons (Fon:"Mino" or "Minon" or "Agoji") were a Fon all-female military regiment of the Kingdom of Dahomey which existed until 1904. 

The British sent diplomatic missions to Dahomey, in an effort to convince King Ghezo to abolish human sacrifice and slave trading.

During their membership they were not allowed to have children or be part of married life (though they were legally married to the king). Many of them were virgins. The regiment had a semi-sacred status, which was intertwined with the Fon belief in Vodun.

Before discussing The Woman King, I will preface my comments with excerpts from a Foreword by Jane I. Guyer and David Easterbrook to the 1958 Edition [Dahomean Narrative—A Cross Cultural Analysis]. 
Melville Jean Herskovits (September 10, 1895 – February 25, 1963) Born to Jewish immigrants in Bellefontaine, Ohio, in 1895, Herskovits attended local public schools. He served in the United States Army Medical Corps in France during World War I. He was an American anthropologist who, according to some, helped establish African and African Diaspora studies in American academia. He is known for exploring the cultural continuity from African cultures as expressed in African-American communities. He worked with his wife Frances (Shapiro) Herskovits, also an anthropologist, in the field in South America, the Caribbean, and Africa. They jointly wrote several books and monographs.

Herskovits’s major work from the trip, the two-volume: “Dahomey, An Ancient West African Kingdom,” [published in 1967, Northwestern University Press] includes a specification of France’s own contribution: All the information pertaining the  women’s side of the culture was gathered by her, and much of the data on religion and art.

Dahomean Narrative published in [1958] at a time when Herskovits was deeply involved in advising the U.S. government on policies toward newly independent African countries, bears her imprint to an even greater degree.

Dahomean Narrative, their collection of the oral tradition of Dahomey, was written in 1931, but not in a formal fashion with a scholarly introduction until 1958. The Narratives consisted of 155 tales spread over nine classifications around everyday life; Exploits of the Gods; Divination; Hunter Stories; Enfant Terrible Tales: twins, orphans, and the abnormally born; Yo Stories; “Historical Tales:”: exploits of the Aladahonu dynasty; Tales of Women: love, intrigue, and betrayal; Explanatory and Moralizing Tales; and Miscellaneous Tales.

Dahomean Narrative published in [1958] at a time when Herskovits was deeply involved in advising the U.S. government on policies toward newly independent African countries, bears her imprint to an even greater degree.

Dahomean Narrative, their collection of the oral tradition of Dahomey, was written in 1931, but not in a formal fashion with a scholarly introduction until 1958. The Narratives consisted of 155 tales spread over nine classifications around everyday life; Exploits of the Gods; Divination; Hunter Stories; Enfant Terrible Tales: twins, orphans, and the abnormally born; Yo Stories; “Historical Tales:”: exploits of the Aladahonu dynasty; Tales of Women: love, intrigue, and betrayal; Explanatory and Moralizing Tales; and Miscellaneous Tales.

When Dahomean Narrative was first published in 1958, Melville J. Herskovits and his collaborator, Francis S. Herskovits had spent over twenty years studying the social networks, religion, music, and oral tradition of the peoples of West Afrika and their descendants in the New World. Dahomey, the major site of their African work, is the country now known as the Republic of Benin.

The time that had elapsed between the gathering of the tales in Dahomean Narrative and their appearance in print has proved to be not without some gains. Subsequent field experiences in the historically related Negro cultures of the New World  (in Haiti, Trinidad, and Brazil) and first hand study of other parts of the African continent have enriched their understanding of the role of the spoken arts in all these cultures.

The Herskovits’s opportunity to revisit Dahomey more than twenty years after their narratives were collected gave them a real sense of the continuities in Dahomean life, of which oral literature is so effective a vehicle. They were able again to meet some of the tellers of the narratives [tales] they presented and learn how they had taken their places in in the higher age-levels of Dahomean society. One, whose favorite tale was No. 110 (Human Ingratitude: Why the races of man differ in color), had become head of the ancestral cult of the royal family. Another, who contributed to the Legba myth, No. 10 (How Legba became guardian of men and gods: Why the dog is respected), was dokpwegan, head of the young men of his community and burial chief. And a third, who had died in the interim, had as the years progressed become the narrator most sought after at the wakes that are so important in the rites of death.

Melville Jean Herskovits  (1895 – 1963) taught at Northwestern University from 1927 until his death. In 1951 he was named to the first academic chair of African studies in the United States. His other major works include The Myth of the Negro past (1941), Man and His Works (1848), The Human Factor in Changing Africa (1962),  and his wife and collaborator, Francis Shapiro Herskovits (1897 – 1972), Rebel Destiny (1934) and Trinidad Village (1947). Jane I. Guyer is director of the Program of African Studies, and David L. Easterbrook is curator of the Melville J. Herskovits Library of African Studies, both at Northwestern University.

Dahomey, An Ancient West African Kingdom ~ Northwestern University Press, 1967 is not on Amazon but it can be found in major libraries. I made copies of substantial portions of it, vol. 1 and 2, at the Schomburg Center for Research In Black Culture while researching for my novel “Leaving Dahomey.”

There was no Woman King in Dahomey. And the name Amazons was given them by the British. European encroachment into west Africa gained pace during the latter half of the 19ty century, and in 1890 King Behanzin started fighting French forces in the course of the First Franco-Dahomean War. They were not fighting the British to preserve slavery because slavery was long over —  they were fighting to defend their homeland. And they won several battles against the French. Ultimately, bolstered by the Foreign Legion, and armed with superior weaponry, including machine guns, as well as calvary  and Marine infantry, the French inflicted casualties that were ten times worse on the Dahomey side. After several more battles the French prevailed.  The Legionnaires later wrote about the “incredible courage and audacity” of the Amazons. The last surviving Amazon [Mino or Ahosi] died in 1979.

The women warriors of Dahomey were referred to as Mino, meaning “Our Mothers” in the Fon language by the male army of Dahomey. The Mino were recruited from among the Ahosi (“king’s wives”). Some women in Fon society became Ahosi voluntarily, while others were involuntarily enrolled if their husbands or fathers complained to the King.  During their membership they were not allowed to have children or be a part of married life. Many of them were virgins. The regiment had a semi-sacred status, which was intertwined with the Fon belief in Vodun. The Mino were rigorously trained, given uniforms, and equipped with Danish guns (obtained via the slave trade). By the 1840s the Mino consisted of between 4000 and 6000 women, about a third of the entire Dahomean Army.

King Houegbadja (who ruled from 1645 to 1685), the third King of Dahomey, is said to have originally started the group which would become as a corps of elephant hunters called the gbeto. Houegbadja’s son King Agadja (ruling from 1708 to 1732) developed the female bodyguard into a militia and successfully used them to defeat the neighboring kingdom of Savi in 1727. European merchants recorded their presence, as well as similar female warriors amongst the Ashanti. For the next hundred years or so, they gained a reputation as fearless warriors. Though they fought rarely, they usually acquitted themselves well in battle.

The Oyo army was a Yoruba empire of what is today western and northern  Nigeria. In 1728, the Oyo Empire invaded the Kingdom of Dahomey in a major and bitter campaign. The force that invaded Dahomey was entirely composed of calvary. Dahomey possessed no calvary (because they had no hoses because of the tsetse fly), but many firearms. These firearms proved effective because in scaring the horses of Oyo’s calvary they were prevented from charging them.

Dahomey’s army also built fortifications such as trenches, which forced the Oyo army to fight as infantry. The battle lasted four days, but the Yoruba were eventually victorious after their reinforcements arrived. Dahomey was forced to pay tribute of their sons and daughters and money to Oyo after the latter’s hard-fought victory. This would not end the fighting, and Yoruba would invade Dahomey a total of seven times before the little kingdom was fully subjugated in 1748.

Gender dysphoria (GD) is the distress a person feels due to a mismatch between their gender identity—their personal sense of their own gender—and their sex assigned at birth. The diagnostic label gender identity disorder (GID) was used until 2013 with the release of the diagnostic manual DSM-5. The condition was renamed to remove the STIGMA (lmao) associated with the term disorder.

Lmao
Body integrity dysphoria (BID, also referred to as body integrity identity disorder, amputee identity disorder and xenomelia, formerly called apotemnophilia) is a mental disorder characterized by a desire to have a sensory or physical disability or having discomfort with being able-bodied beginning in early adolescence and resulting in harmful consequences. BID appears to be related to somatoparaphrenia. People with this condition may refer to themselves as "transabled".

A woman is an adult female human. Prior to adulthood, a female human is referred to as a girl (a female child or adolescent). The plural women is sometimes used in certain phrases such as "women's rights" to denote female humans regardless of age. Denoting the sex that can bear offspring or produce eggs, distinguished biologically by the production of gametes (ova) which can be fertilized by male gametes.

A man is an adult male human.
Prior to adulthood, a male human is referred to as a boy (a male child or adolescent). 
Like most other male mammals, a man's genome inherits an X chromosome from the mother and a Y chromosome from the father. Sex differentiation of the male fetus is governed by the SRY gene on the Y chromosome. During puberty, hormones which stimulate androgen production result in the development of secondary sexual characteristics, thus exhibiting greater differences between the sexes. These include greater muscle mass, the growth of facial hair and a lower body fat composition.

The origins of Dahomey can be traced back to the Fon people of the interior of the African continent, who banded together in a conglomerate in order to oppose the political authority of the Yoruba People of Oyo. Technically an official subject of the Yoruba of Oyo, the Fon people were forced to pay tribute to their political conquerors and were subjected to cavalry raids made by the Oyo armies in order to supply the slave trade.

This unusual emergence of an all-female military regiment was the result of Dahomey's male population facing high casualties in frequent warfare with neighboring West African states, as well as Dahomey being forced to annually give male slaves to the Oyo Empire.

The Oyo Empire and Kingdom of Dahomey have a long and complex history. Hopefully, it shows the evil and the good from all sides.

The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves. As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organized for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol. Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

WIKI:

"Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland.[30] The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey.[30]

Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey."

Ignore the part where they claim this is inspired by a true story. The fights they are referring to happened in the 1890s. Both sides had guns and the Africans were NOT dressed like that. The Europeans were also using steam ships by this point, not sailing ships. Also, their enemies were the French, not the English. The English did blockade the country in the 1840s while trying to enforce a prohibition on slavery but that didn't result in a land war. As mentioned by others the Dahomey had been made wealthy by being slave traders, were known for regularly raiding neighboring villages and tribes for the purpose of taking slaves, and were still doing so in 1890. They also routinely used their slaves for human sacrifices during the "Annual Customs of Dahomey". Lastly, and SPOILER ALERT, they lose to the French despite the Dahomey Amazons having "handled admirably" and their "incredible courage and audacity" according to the French.

sidenote for actual fans of historical accuracy: slavery in Africa (unlike the slavery that Europeans popularized) was not based on race nor did it promote the idea of a “permanent slave class.” Slavery has existed in EVERY “major” civilization known to man. Typically, the enslaved were people who from conquered nations. African nations did not approach slavery as a system that was supposed to span for multiple generations or lock people into an a permanent state-sanctioned apartheid. Africans sold other Africans into the slave trade with the assumption that those people would be part of a temporary systemcass —not a permanent apartheid. White Europeans are the ones who introduced the idea of slavery as a permanent racial caste system.

As an African history enthusiast I have a few problems with the film overall, there are red flags I'm seeing. Firstly title and the direction overall is concerning. Viola Davis' character is no king or monarch she is a general so why call her king? These warriors did exist in the kingdom of Dahomey for those who don't know  but, they did not fight Europeans until much in the Franco-Dahomey wars and got beat horribly.  Before this when Europeans arrived they traded slaves with Europeans. Also a sad fact about king Gezo who is played by John Boyega is he was a tyrant. Gezo got into power by usurping with the support of European slave merchants in the port city of Ouidah. Gezo also tried to convince the British to continue the slave trade after they set up blockade so no ships could leave. If they were showcasing the complex nature and relationship of this history I'd be fine with it but theyre over simplifying it. Plus they could have called the film Daughters of Dahomey, or Our Mothers (as they were known as in the Fon language of the Dahomey) but Woman King?? Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland.[30] The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey.[30]

Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey.[6]

The Kingdom of Dahomey was an important regional power that had an organized domestic economy built on conquest and slave labor...

The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves. As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organized for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol. Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

For the uninitiated ... there were literally two wars against the French (in which, despite eventually losing to French military weapons), the Amazons of Dahomey were revered by the world press as extremely unique and fearless warriors. This particular battalion in the Kingdom of Dahomey existed WELL over 2 centuries before the wars against the French occurred.

 The Dahomey Amazon warriors were first recruited in the late 1600s by King Hougebadja and declined in 1904. Many surviving female warriors continued to have families, many were said to secretly surround Agoli-Agbo (pretending to be his wives) after the exile of his brother - the famous King Behanzin, a legend in Benin today - by order of the French colonists. King Behanzin sent to Martinique in the Caribbean with his royal court and son, who later became a lawyer in France.

Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland.[30] The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey.[30]

Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

If  Anyone thinks this is based on history you've got another think coming.. Europeans did not go into the continent, except for missionaries and explorers until the 1800s (well after the slave trade was abolished).  Those that did venture into the continent we're plagued with yellow fever, malaria and other tropical diseases and spent more time in bed than they actually did traveling.  The slave trade was founded on Africans capturing each other,  from neighboring tribes and taking them to the coast and selling them in large slave markets. They were the lucky ones, because other slaves captured were generally treated brutally, used as sacrifices, even sacrificed for the death of the king of the tribe. They would even go to war  specifically to kill people for the afterlife after life of the king because they believed whoever you killed became your king's servant in the afterlife.  All women belonged to the king  and their favors were  dispensed out to loyal followers.  There was a few tribes that had women Warriors, but none of them fought the Europeans. These women solders were often more blood thirsty than the men. Africa was in a constant state of conflict and there were more slaves in Africa then there were free men.

These comments .... The Franco-Dahomean wars were in the 1890s - years after many/most nations had already abolished slavery. These wars were fought over COLONIALISM - not slavery. Suggesting that the French fought the Dahomey to end slavery when the French at this time was extorting $$$ Haiti for daring to be free is disgusting revisionist history. These women fought bravely against their would be colonial oppressors and their contribution to history should not be erased because the kingdom had a history of selling prisoners of wars to those who practiced chattel slavery. It should also be mentioned that Dahomey prisoners of wars were ALSO sold as slaves to places like the US, Jamaica and Brazil. This was the nature of the times in several parts of the world. This kingdom should not be held responsible for how or what chattel slavery was - that should fall squarely on the governments that held my ancestors in bondage for generations.

The inspiration for this movie comes from the Kingdom of Dahomey.  Here is an excerpt from the Wikipedia page.

"The Kingdom of Dahomey was an important regional power that had an organized domestic economy built on conquest and slave labor,[3] significant international trade and diplomatic relations with Europeans, a centralized administration, taxation systems, and an organized military. Notable in the kingdom were significant artwork, an all-female military unit called the Dahomey Amazons by European observers, and the elaborate religious practices of Vodun.[4]

The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves.[2] As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organized for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol.
Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.[2][6]"

I’ve seen some comments  about this not being historically accurate  . First of all I am not justifying that kingdoms that participated in slavery are not at fault but the story is far more complicated . Although  Dahomey is one of the kingdoms that heavily participated in the slave trade , let’s not cast the Europeans as the good guys here . The British , French and the rest of the other slave trading European nations and those who benefited from selling the craft of slavery created a situation in Africa where you as a kingdom either participated in the slave trade or you became enslaved which most of them ended up being in the end . Slavery in Africa was basically an arms race . Europe produced guns that they exchanged for slaves , in order to get guns the only thing  the Europeans enslavers would take were people . So you had all these guns on the continent that were being used to subjugate and enslave . If you as a kingdom did not have the latest guns your people would end up as ship cargo heading to the americas. This was such insidious scheming by Europe  . Out of this you had Dahomey which was an extreme warrior cast society , you can closely compare them to the Spartans , they were basically bred for war . So the institution of European slave trade  for centuries basically made them one of the most formidable forces  on the African continent due to the wealth they gained from participating in European slave trade . At the end of the day when the next phase of European domination of Africa  started (colonialism) , the Europeans had to face a formidable force that they had helped build . But the colonizer Europeans wanted their land and to enslave them in their own land(colonization) , this is the time period  this whole story more than likely comes from. Please don’t sit there and act like Europe was the one trying to put an end to slavery , they basically used that new excuse of ending slavery  to go to war with the Dahomey to enslave them in colonialism and take their land and resources  . Same thing happened to the Ashanti Kingdom . They held up for a century before they were defeated and their cities burned to the ground to make way for the British extracting unimaginable gold and other resources out of the continent . You should read the book “ how Europe underdeveloped Africa” to get a better understanding . Ok I’m off my soap box , have an awesome day

In the trailer, we will talk about the period 1818 - 1858, the reign of King Gezo (Boeg). It was a period of the most brutal slave trade, Dahomey ran into neighboring countries and took away a bunch of slaves from there to be sold to Europeans, as well as to palm oil plantations.
Then the British sailed to them, they wanted to convince them to stop the slave trade. But Gezo refused. As a result, after the prohibition of the slave trade in Europe and the United States, Dahomey continued to conquer heaps of slaves, but not for sale, but on palm oil plantations.
That is, the richest income from the kingdom was from the slave trade, and when it was banned, from the sale of palm oil, which was grown by the same slaves.

HOWEVER, in the trailer it is clearly seen that blacks are chopping whites. The war with France began only in 1890, that is, 30 years after the death of Gezo, which is discussed in the film. So they are not French.
Then who? The British mission that prevented the slave trade? In Britain, since 1833, slavery was prohibited, so there was no point in buying slaves on an industrial scale.

It seems to me that the creators will proudly remain silent about the fact that 90% of their economy was in the slave trade and forced labor on plantations. But they will bring in evil whites who will force poor black children to trade in slaves.
And I don't see armed forces, except for women. In fact, the Amazon corps was only a small part of it, the regular army consisted of men, and not with spears and bows, but with guns, since they sold slaves for 200 years in exchange for firearms, thanks to which they raided neighboring countries and sold slaves from there.

Both domestic slavery and the Atlantic slave trade were important to the economy of Dahomey. Men, women, and children captured by Dahomey in wars and slave raids were sold to European slave traders in exchange for various goods such as rifles, gunpowder, textiles, cowry shells, and alcohol. Dahomey used magical rituals for slave trading. Prior to being sold to Europeans, slaves were forced to march in circles around the "Tree of Forgetfulness" so they would lose memories of their culture, family, and homeland.[30] The purpose of this ritual was to prevent the spirits of the slaves from returning and seeking revenge against the royalty of Dahomey.[30]

Other war captives who were not intended to be sold to Europeans remained in Dahomey as slaves. There, they worked on royal plantations that supplied food for the army and royal court, and they were reserved for human sacrifice in the Annual Customs of Dahomey.[6]

According to what I was just reading, this is based on the Dahomey Amazons, a military branch in what is now Benin. 

Although they fought the British and French, ironically, one of their main causes for war is that their economy depended greatly on the Atlantic Slave Trade which the British were trying to put a stop to. 

The Amazons existed because they were vassals to the nearby Yoruba of the Oyo Empire who demanded tributes of able-bodied men which left the Dahomey military a little short of troops.

The growth of Dahomey coincided with the growth of the Atlantic slave trade, and it became known to Europeans as a major supplier of slaves. As a highly militaristic kingdom constantly organised for warfare, it captured children, women, and men during wars and raids against neighboring societies, and sold them into the Atlantic slave trade in exchange for European goods such as rifles, gunpowder, fabrics, cowrie shells, tobacco, pipes, and alcohol. Other remaining captives became slaves in Dahomey, where they worked on royal plantations and were routinely mass executed in large-scale human sacrifices during the festival celebrations known as the Annual Customs of Dahomey.

Above all else, I hope people understand, that we don’t get movies that reflect African civilizations and religion in mainstream film. In mainstream cinema it is usually European or Asian kingdoms that are explored, & when it is Africa it is always a Egypt devoid of Africans or Africa devoid of humans with talking animals. With that said, the Nubian Kingdoms of Kush & their regnant-queens, would actually fit the title of “woman king”. In most Fon kingdoms the queen-mother, first wife of the king & other wives didn’t have much power. But this is Hollywood they turn most historical things, into dramas, so there is expected to be allot of creative liberty. As an AFricanAmerican I hope this opens the flood gates for more films about African diaspora rebellions, quilombos, & African civilizations. Africa’s history is global history. Peace. 
Edit: Africans were like Native Americans in that they were not a single group (duh). Pan-continent/ pan-racial unity was not a concept yet.
